target: data/sections.json part1 SEC-1.8 — add n(r) empirical fitting defense to other side section
insert_after: The dome’s n(r) formula contains no wavelength dependence — it is a single scalar function of radial position.
replacement_html: A deeper version of the self-consistency defense: n(r) was designed to reproduce correct Polaris elevations — the match to globe-latitude values is the aetheric scaling law working as intended. Empirical fitting is legitimate science; Newton fitted G to observations before predicting orbits. The question is always whether a fitted model predicts beyond its calibration domain. The dome’s n(r) was calibrated against one observable (Polaris elevation angles). But if n(r) correctly describes how the aetheric medium refracts Polaris starlight, the same function must also describe how it refracts sunlight — and here the model collapses. A local sun at 5,733 km observed through n(r) produces 52% angular diameter variation across the day (Section 2.4), while observations show constancy to 0.01%. Fitting Polaris while destroying solar observations is not “the scaling law working” — it is overfitting to one observable. The dome’s n(r) is not an independent physical prediction; it is a parameterized fit to the thing it claims to replace, and the first out-of-sample test it faces, it fails by a factor of 5,200.
holes_addressed: Missing defense preemption: n(r) empirically fitted to reproduce correct Polaris elevations (curmudgeon SEC-1.8-post-EXP129.c2.json, moderate severity)
new_evidence_added: Cross-reference to Section 2.4 solar diameter analysis (52% predicted vs 0.01% observed)
Overfitting framing: calibration domain vs out-of-sample prediction failure

anticipated_objections.objection: The 52% variation assumes no refraction correction for sunlight — perhaps a separate solar n(r) accounts for it.
anticipated_objections.response: The dome defines a single n(r) function with no wavelength or source dependence. Invoking a separate solar correction is ad hoc and not in the model. Even if added, it would need to compress 52% variation to 0.01% — a precision challenge the dome has never attempted.

anticipated_objections.objection: Newton also had parameters that failed outside their domain before refinement — this is how science iterates.
anticipated_objections.response: True, but Newton published the refinements. The dome has not acknowledged the solar diameter problem, let alone fixed n(r) to accommodate it. Moreover, the failure mode is not a small deviation requiring fine-tuning — it is a 5,200× overshoot.

anticipated_objections.objection: Section 2.4's 52% figure assumes a physical sun at 5,733 km, but the dome says that altitude is an optical illusion.
anticipated_objections.response: This creates a dilemma. If the sun is NOT at 5,733 km (illusion), the dome has no stated physical altitude, and every sun-dependent prediction lacks a physical basis. If the sun IS at 5,733 km (real), the 52% variation applies. Either way, n(r) fails.

kernel_of_truth: Empirical fitting IS legitimate science. The Ptolemaic epicycles were fitted to observations and predicted eclipses accurately. The n(r) approach of calibrating against Polaris and then predicting other observables is methodologically sound in principle. The problem is not the method — it is that the very first out-of-sample test (solar diameter) fails catastrophically, revealing n(r) as a single-observable overfit rather than a physical law.
cross_references: WIN-065 (n(r) formula — note cross-WIN consistency issue flagged by curmudgeon)
notes: Paragraph inserted after the wavelength-selectivity defense and before the future-ray-trace defense. This maintains the escalating structure: each defense is stronger than the previous, and the rebuttal is correspondingly more specific. The empirical-fitting defense is philosophically the strongest (it concedes overfitting as a risk but frames the dome as doing normal science), so the solar diameter cross-reference is the most devastating rebuttal — it shows the overfit is not hypothetical but measured.
